dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for a 71-year-old female experiencing shortness of breath at her residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Nancy, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, I've had trouble breathing for about an hour. It hasn't improved with my inhaler.
Let me just ask you a few quick questions to assess your alertness. What is your name?
My name is Karen.
Can you tell me where you are right now?
We are in [CITY] County.
Is there anything in your airway that makes it feel blocked or difficult to breathe?
No. I'm just breathing very hard.
Her respiratory rate is very fast - about 27 breaths per minute. It's shallow.
You seem to be working hard to breathe. Can you tell me when you first noticed this?
Her oxygen saturation is 94%, so it's not critically low, but we'll monitor closely.
I hear air trapping in the lungs - that's common in COPD. Have you felt like your chest is tight?
Her pulse is 102 beats per minute and strong.
Her blood pressure is 227 over 106. That's significantly elevated.
You mentioned trouble breathing. Do you have any symptoms like chest tightness or nausea?
Family reports that you have a history of COPD and primary hypertension. Is that true?
Yes, and I also have chronic pancreatitis, depression, and alcohol use.
And your age, Karen?
I'm 71 years old.
We'll do a quick check. Have you experienced any pain today?
No pain at all. Just very short of breath.
I'll recheck your pulse again now. It's still elevated.
Let's get your blood pressure again. It's still quite elevated.
And your breathing was 27 per minute earlier. I'll take that again now.
I need to check your pupils for reactivity. Can you look this way while I shine a light? ... Great, your pupils are reactive and equal in size.
Your skin appears normal in color, but it's warm and sweaty - this is called diaphoresis.
Let me check your orientation. Can you tell me today's date?
… And are you aware of the people here and your location?
I'm going to listen to your breathing. We're hearing decreased breath sounds on both sides with air trapping.
I'm placing you on 4 liters per minute of oxygen via nasal cannula now to help.
I will now check your blood glucose levels. It's part of our routine checks.
I'm going to perform a 4-lead EKG to get a look at your heart rhythm.
Based on your breathing and vitals, we'll follow the respiratory distress protocol for COPD exacerbation.
Your pulse has increased now to 129 beats per minute. That's still a big increase.
Your respiratory rate is now at 33 breaths per minute - that's much higher.
Your blood pressure is 231 over 110 - it's still high, Kar'n.
We've given you a terbutaline shot to help relieve some of the breathing difficulty.
Your saturation has now decreased to 92% - so we're going to increase the oxygen to 15 liters per minute with a non-rebreather.
We performed a 12-lead EKG and found a normal rhythm with no significant heart issues.
I also measured your ETCO2 and found it to be 50 mmHg. That's borderline, but it's not a concern right now.
As per protocol, we have completed the assessments and will proceed with transport.
We'll be taking you to [COMPANY_NAME] today. It's the best place for further evaluation of your breathing.
Our crew is preparing to move you from the upstairs room down using a stair chair. You'll be safely fastened.
Just confirming - you're still alert and oriented. A&Ox4 all the way.
Karen, are you still aware of what's going on, who is here, and what time it is?
Yes, I think I am.
Your EKG report showed sinus tachycardia but no significant ST changes that we should worry about now.
Our team has secured you on the stretcher and will transport you with continuous oxygen support.
The ED at [COMPANY_NAME] is aware we're coming and will be prepared for your arrival.
Let's double-check - you're still A&Ox4 all four categories.
Your pulse is 129 now, still elevated, but we're transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for additional treatment support.
We're full go for transport. We'll keep you monitored throughout the ride and update them at the hospital.
